video_title: The AI Investment Boom: When Will It Pay Off?
gist: Goldman's Jim Covello (head of global equity research) and George Lee (co-head of Global Institute) revisit Covello's 2024 AI skepticism two years on: consumer adoption and the technology exceeded expectations, but enterprise ROI remains unproven, and Covello is now as or more skeptical on the economics. All AI value still accrues to semiconductors at the expense of every layer above; Covello now prefers hyperscaler stocks over semis, expecting them to outperform in two of three forward scenarios.

themes.theme: AI capex vs. returns — the unproven economics

insights.claim: Goldman's Jim Covello says he is as or more skeptical on AI economics today than two years ago, despite acknowledging the technology and consumer adoption have exceeded his expectations.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello (Goldman head of global equity research)
insights.so_what: A senior Goldman research voice staying bearish on AI ROI after two years is a contrarian signal against the bullish equity positioning in AI names.
insights.quote: I'm probably as or more skeptical on the economics today than I was before, despite how incredible the technology is.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers raised AI capex rather than cutting it even after their stocks underperformed due to capex-driven free-cash-flow pressure — the opposite of what Goldman predicted, which Covello says calls the economics into question even more.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Capex discipline is not acting as the expected circuit-breaker; investors cannot rely on stock underperformance to force a spending slowdown.
insights.quote: instead of cutting the capex, they've actually raised the capex.

insights.claim: Goldman estimates roughly $7 to $8 trillion will be spent on the AI build-out, and George Lee argues that summing up disruption of existing profit pools sector by sector still falls short of justifying that spend.
insights.speaker: George Lee (co-head, Goldman Sachs Global Institute)
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete payback bar: disrupting incumbents alone can't fund AI; the bull case requires entirely new economic activity (new TAMs) that may not materialize.
insights.quote: you'd still fall short of a significant enough payoff from what we think will be $7 to $8 trillion spent here.

insights.claim: Covello says companies are losing more money implementing AI today than two years ago, so the hill to a payoff is steeper now because more capital has been sunk.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: The breakeven threshold rises with cumulative spend; later returns must be larger to justify the investment, raising the risk of disappointment.
insights.quote: companies are losing more money today implementing this technology than they were two years ago.

insights.claim: Covello frames the entire AI investment debate as resolving to one test — whether enterprises make or save money implementing AI — and warns that if the same 'it's early' debate persists in two years, the thesis is in trouble.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Gives investors a single decisive KPI to track (enterprise profit/savings from AI) and a roughly two-year window before patience should run out.
insights.quote: it all boils down to one thing: do the enterprises make or save money implementing AI?

themes.theme: Semis capture all the value — the chain must rebalance

insights.claim: Covello says this AI cycle is unprecedented because semiconductor companies are thriving at the economic expense of everyone above them in the chain, unlike past cycles where chipmakers thrived only when their customers thrived.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello (covered semiconductor stocks for 16 years)
insights.so_what: Flags an unsustainable value distribution: the profit concentration in chips is anomalous and historically self-correcting, implying eventual mean reversion.
insights.quote: Here in this cycle, the semiconductor companies are thriving at the economic expense of everybody above them in the chain.

insights.claim: Covello argues the semiconductor profit concentration must eventually rectify — either every layer above starts generating profit, or chip spending gets scaled back — and says it can't go on forever even though it has lasted longer than he expected.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Two-sided risk for semis: either broadening profits validate the chain, or a capex pullback hits chip demand directly.
insights.quote: at some point that has to rectify itself.

insights.claim: Goldman called for focusing on AI 'picks and shovels' two years ago expecting early value to accrue to semiconductors, and Covello says that call played out correctly.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Confirms the infrastructure-first trade worked, but Covello signals the next leg requires a rotation away from that thesis.
insights.quote: we should focus on the picks and shovels because that's where we think the economic value is going to accrue in the beginning. That did play out.

insights.claim: Covello says market positioning is incredibly bullish on semis and bearish on everything below them in the chain, and that this positioning has to turn around at some point.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Crowded long-semis positioning is itself a risk; a rotation could punish consensus semi longs and reward the under-owned layers above.
insights.quote: The positioning of the market is incredibly bullish on semis. And bearing on everything below that. At some point that has to turn around.

themes.theme: Hyperscalers over semis — Covello's new trade

insights.claim: Covello now favors hyperscaler stocks over semiconductor stocks — a reversal from two years ago — arguing hyperscalers outperform semis in two of three forward scenarios over the next couple of years.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: An explicit pairs-trade view from Goldman's equity research head: long hyperscalers / underweight semis on a multi-year horizon.
insights.quote: I would favor the hyperscaler stocks today over the semiconductor stocks. Which is a change from two years ago.

insights.claim: In Covello's framework, hyperscaler stocks outperform if enterprises start generating returns (value flows through the whole chain) and also if hyperscalers moderate capex to claw back free cash flow; semis only keep outperforming if the status quo of chip-only profits persists.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Maps three scenarios to a trade: two favor hyperscalers, making the risk/reward skewed toward owning hyperscalers over semis.
insights.quote: I think the hyperscaler stocks will outperform the semiconductor stocks in two of those three scenarios.

insights.claim: Covello clarifies the capex-moderation scenario is not hyperscalers going from $200 billion a year to zero, but a slight moderation of the pace to recover free cash flow.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Quantifies the hyperscaler capex baseline (~$200B/yr) and frames a soft-landing path that would be bullish for hyperscaler equity without collapsing chip demand entirely.
insights.quote: We're not talking about companies going from 200 billion a year in capex to zero.

insights.claim: Covello warns that if markets or the economy hit a rough patch, the AI spending the market tolerates in a good environment could get scaled back — and notes a moderation might not be a bad thing for hyperscaler stocks.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Links macro/market downturn risk to AI capex fragility — a watch item where a market wobble could trigger the capex-moderation scenario.
insights.quote: if we did hit more of a rough patch in the markets or the economy, some of this spending that the market is okay doing in a good environment might get scaled back a little bit.

insights.claim: George Lee says aggregated public revenue from independent AI model companies reached in roughly three years a level that took the cloud industry about 15-17 years to achieve, making them the fastest-growing companies in corporate history on the top line.
insights.speaker: George Lee
insights.so_what: Quantifies the bull case: top-line growth of model companies is historically unprecedented even if profits remain elusive.
insights.quote: It took something like 15, 16, 17 years for the Cloud companies in aggregate to get there.

insights.claim: Lee says coding is the best application for AI because it is a verifiable domain, and the strong product-market fit in agentic coding only really took off at the end of last year; extending that fit to other domains will be harder.
insights.speaker: George Lee
insights.so_what: Identifies coding as the proven monetizable use case and flags that ROI in non-verifiable domains is the harder, still-unproven frontier.
insights.quote: coding is probably the best application for this technology. It is a verifiable domain.

themes.theme: Enterprise adoption friction — data, scale, and ROI measurement

insights.claim: Covello says many enterprises are deploying agents on top of data that 'isn't ready to be agented,' creating poor or incomplete query results and an additional economic drag because organizational data doesn't speak to itself.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Points to data-readiness/integration vendors as a gating bottleneck and a potential investment opportunity before agentic ROI can materialize.
insights.quote: the data isn't ready to be agented yet. So, we're putting agents on top of data that isn't ready to be agented.

insights.claim: Lee argues AI productivity gains show up most in young 'AI-native' companies built from the jump around the technology, while large incumbents face a 'drag coefficient' from legacy systems, enshrined workflows, and worker resistance.
insights.speaker: George Lee
insights.so_what: Suggests disruptive upside accrues to AI-native challengers near-term, with incumbent productivity gains lagging and uncertain in timing.
insights.quote: Where you can see the real takeoff in productivity of these technologies is in very young companies, call them new native, AI native companies

insights.claim: Lee argues even an AI believer must reckon with the risk that the enterprise advantage from deploying AI is fleeting — competitors catch up and margin gains get competed away, with surplus potentially passing through to consumers as in prior tech waves.
insights.speaker: George Lee
insights.so_what: Even successful AI deployment may not durably lift margins, undermining the equity case that AI adopters will sustainably out-earn peers.
insights.quote: will the margin advantages get competed away?

insights.claim: Covello and Lee frame AI spending as a game-theoretic arms race: AI capex is increasingly the cost of doing business, where not investing means permanent margin disadvantage, making scale a massive advantage as surprise model-upgrade costs (e.g. a sudden $50 million spend) crowd out smaller players.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello / George Lee
insights.so_what: Game-theory dynamics sustain AI spending regardless of ROI and entrench large incumbents — bullish for scaled players, bearish for sub-scale firms.
insights.quote: if I don't make these investments, I'm in a permanent margin disadvantage.

insights.claim: Covello cites third-party surveys consistently showing a gap between C-suite expectations of AI's productivity impact and the lower benefit line workers actually report, partly attributable to fragmented organizational data.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: The expectations-vs-reality productivity gap is a leading indicator to watch for whether enterprise AI ROI is being overstated by management.
insights.quote: the line workers aren't getting as much benefit from it as the C suite expected.

themes.theme: Second-order risks — bull-market dependence and AI backlash

insights.claim: Covello calls this the best bull market of his lifetime and says it is circular — AI is the reason for the bull market, and the bull market is powering the AI investment — implying AI capex is partly dependent on continued equity strength.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Reflexivity risk: a market downturn could undercut the AI capex that is itself sustaining the rally, creating a potential negative feedback loop.
insights.quote: this is the best bull market of our lifetime... This technology is the reason for it. And that bull market is powering a lot of the investment that's happening.

insights.claim: Lee flags a sudden turn toward populist resentment of AI — booing at commencements, violence tied to data-center deployment — that he says is almost uniquely strong in the US and could slow AI progress, potentially coming to a head at the midterm elections.
insights.speaker: George Lee
insights.so_what: Political/social backlash is an emerging US-specific headwind on data-center buildout and AI deployment; the midterms are a catalyst date to watch.
insights.quote: We're getting to a point where this is a deeply unpopular technology. Interestingly, almost uniquely in the US versus other parts of the world.

insights.claim: Covello widens the economics question to whether individuals benefit economically from AI enough to offset rising electricity costs, tying AI's social license to the distribution of returns across the supply chain.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: Frames power costs and consumer benefit as part of the AI ROI ledger — a watch item for utilities, power demand, and political durability of the buildout.
insights.quote: how much of it is going to flow back to the individual?

insights.claim: Covello advises investors not to blindly assume AI will pay off nor that it definitely won't, but to track specific technical and economic markers along the way to gauge the most likely outcome.
insights.speaker: Jim Covello
insights.so_what: A disciplined, milestone-based approach to the AI trade rather than binary conviction — useful framing for sizing AI exposure.
insights.quote: I don't think investors should just be blindly assuming it's going to happen. I think you need to question along the way.
